pullRequest.title: docs: use utilsforecast.evaluation.evaluate in notebooks
pullRequest.body:
Closes #834

This PR replaces custom `evaluate_performance` functions and direct metric calls (e.g., `mse()`, `mae()`) with the standardized `utilsforecast.evaluation.evaluate` function across documentation notebooks.

### Changes

- **getting_Started_complete.ipynb**
    - Added `NIXTLA_ID_AS_COL=1` env var to ensure `unique_id` is a column (not index)
    - Replaced custom `evaluate_cv` implementation with `evaluate(df, metrics=[metric], models=models)`

- **getting_Started_complete_polars.ipynb**
    - Same `evaluate()` migration (polars already returns `unique_id` as column)

- **GARCH_tutorial.ipynb**
    - Added `NIXTLA_ID_AS_COL=1`
    - Replaced `mae(cv_df, models=models)` with `evaluate(cv_df, metrics=[mae], models=models)`

- **CrossValidation.ipynb**
    - Added `NIXTLA_ID_AS_COL=1`
    - Replaced `rmse(cv_df, models=['AutoETS'])` with `evaluate(cv_df, metrics=[rmse], models=['AutoETS'], agg_fn='mean')`

### Why `NIXTLA_ID_AS_COL`?

The `cross_validation()` and `forecast()` methods currently return DataFrames with `unique_id` as the index. The `evaluate()` function expects `unique_id` as a column. Setting this env var ensures consistent behavior and prepares for the future default where `unique_id` will be a column.

### Testing

- [x] `getting_Started_complete.ipynb` passes nbdev_test
- [x] Polars notebook unchanged (already compatible)
- [x] Tutorial notebooks have `.notest` but changes follow same pattern

pullRequest.title: [FEAT] Add python 3.13
pullRequest.body: - Adds Python 3.13 to supported Python versions

pullRequest.title: feat: add UCM (Unobserved Components Model) support
pullRequest.body:
# Feature: Add UCM (Unobserved Components Model) Support

Closes #1012

## Summary

This PR adds support for Unobserved Components Models (UCM), also known as Structural Time Series Models. UCM is a well-known and proven forecasting approach that decomposes time series into interpretable components.

## What is UCM?

UCM (Harvey, 1989) decomposes a time series into:
- **Level** - intercept that can vary over time
- **Trend** - slope that can vary over time  
- **Seasonal** - periodic patterns
- **Cycle** - longer-term oscillations
- **Irregular** - noise

This is similar to classical decomposition but uses state-space methods and the Kalman filter, allowing for:
- Stochastic (time-varying) components
- Proper uncertainty quantification
- Missing data handling
- Exogenous variables

## Implementation

This PR adds a wrapper around `statsmodels.tsa.statespace.structural.UnobservedComponents` that follows the statsforecast API.

### New Classes

1. **`UCM`** - Full UCM model with all configuration options
2. **`LocalLevel`** - Convenience class for random walk model
3. **`LocalLinearTrend`** - Convenience class for level + trend model
4. **`SmoothTrend`** - Convenience class for integrated random walk
5. **`RandomWalkWithDrift`** - Convenience class for RW with drift

### API

```python
from statsforecast.models import UCM, LocalLevel, LocalLinearTrend

# Basic usage
model = UCM(level='local level', seasonal=12)
model.fit(y)
forecast = model.predict(h=12, level=[90, 95])

# Convenience classes
model = LocalLinearTrend(seasonal=12)

# With StatsForecast
sf = StatsForecast(
    models=[
        LocalLevel(seasonal=12),
        LocalLinearTrend(seasonal=12),
        UCM(level='smooth trend', seasonal=12),
    ],
    freq='MS',
)
sf.fit(df)
forecast = sf.predict(h=12, level=[90])
```

### Features

- ✅ Full statsforecast API compatibility (`fit`, `predict`, `forecast`, `predict_in_sample`)
- ✅ Prediction intervals via statsmodels state-space methods
- ✅ Exogenous variables support
- ✅ Component extraction (`get_components()`)
- ✅ Multiple level/trend specifications (local level, local linear trend, smooth trend, etc.)
- ✅ Seasonal component with configurable period
- ✅ Cycle and autoregressive components
- ✅ Works with StatsForecast parallelization

## Testing

```python
import numpy as np
from statsforecast import StatsForecast
from statsforecast.models import UCM, LocalLevel, LocalLinearTrend

# Create data with trend and seasonality
np.random.seed(42)
n = 120
t = np.arange(n)
y = 50 + 0.5 * t + 10 * np.sin(2 * np.pi * t / 12) + np.random.randn(n) * 2

# Test models
for Model in [LocalLevel, LocalLinearTrend]:
    model = Model(seasonal=12)
    model.fit(y)
    fc = model.predict(h=12, level=[95])
    print(f"{model}: {fc['mean'][:3]}")
```

## Dependencies

- `statsmodels` (already an optional dependency for some models)

## Checklist

- [x] Implements requested feature (#1012)
- [x] Follows statsforecast API conventions
- [x] Includes comprehensive docstrings
- [x] Works with StatsForecast parallelization
- [x] Handles both pandas and numpy returns from statsmodels
- [x] Convenience classes for common use cases

## References

- Harvey, A. C. (1989). *Forecasting, Structural Time Series Models and the Kalman Filter*
- [statsmodels UCM documentation](https://www.statsmodels.org/stable/generated/statsmodels.tsa.statespace.structural.UnobservedComponents.html)

pullRequest.title: [FEAT] Simulate method for sample trajectories
pullRequest.body:
## Description
This PR significantly expands the [simulate](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/core.py:465:4-505:68) method capabilities across the [StatsForecast](cci:2://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/core.py:1704:0-1957:9) library. Beyond providing basic sample trajectories, it now supports a wide range of error distributions, enabling high-fidelity probabilistic forecasting and robust scenario analysis. 

This implementation centralizes simulation logic, improves model accuracy by incorporating historical residuals, and ensures high consistency across the model library.

## Key Changes
- **Advanced Error Distributions**:
    - Introduced a centralized [statsforecast/simulation.py](cci:7://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/simulation.py:0:0-0:0) utility for sampling from multiple distributions: `normal`, `t` (Student's t), `bootstrap` (empirical), `laplace`, `skew-normal`, and `ged` (Generalized Error Distribution).
    - Implemented calibrated variance scaling for all distributions to ensure generated noise matches the model's estimated [sigma](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/tbats.py:1014:0-1030:17).
- **Expanded Model Coverage**:
    - **Smoothing Models**: Added [simulate](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/core.py:465:4-505:68) methods and analytic prediction intervals to [SimpleExponentialSmoothing](cci:2://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/models.py:2243:0-2426:18) (Standard & Optimized) and [SeasonalExponentialSmoothing](cci:2://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/models.py:2650:0-2851:18) (Standard & Optimized).
    - **Averaging & Drift Models**: Implemented [simulate](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/core.py:465:4-505:68) for [HistoricAverage](cci:2://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/models.py:3161:0-3377:18), [RandomWalkWithDrift](cci:2://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/models.py:3637:0-3857:18), and [WindowAverage](cci:2://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/models.py:4132:0-4257:18).
    - **Stored Residuals**: Updated [fit](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/models.py:1556:4-1571:19) methods across all models to calculate and store `residuals` and [sigma](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/tbats.py:1014:0-1030:17), enabling non-parametric `bootstrap` simulation support.
- **Improved API & Core Engine**:
    - Enhanced `StatsForecast.simulate` and `GroupedArray.simulate` to propagate `error_distribution` and `error_params` through the hierarchy.
    - Full support for parallel execution ([n_jobs](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/core.py:531:0-536:39)) with reproducible seeding for all distributions.

## Verification
- **Automated Tests**: Added [tests/test_simulation_distributions.py](cci:7://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/tests/test_simulation_distributions.py:0:0-0:0) covering:
    - High-level [StatsForecast](cci:2://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/core.py:1704:0-1957:9) API simulations.
    - Individual model-level [simulate](cci:1://file:///Users/ankithemantlade/Desktop/nixtla_statsforecast/python/statsforecast/core.py:465:4-505:68) implementations.
    - Reproducibility across seeds and parallel execution.
    - Bootstrap accuracy using historical residuals.
- **Test Execution**: Ran `pytest tests/test_simulation_distributions.py` — **All 5 tests passed.**

## Checklist
- [x] I have followed the [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/en/v1.0.0/) style for commit messages.
- [x] I have added docstrings following the Google style.
- [x] I have added tests to cover my changes.
- [x] I have verified that all tests pass.
- [x] I have run `ruff` to ensure compliance with the project's style.

pullRequest.title: Unpin scipy
pullRequest.body:
Unpins scipy and update statsmodels accordingly.
Solves #1048 

There were some issues while doing the tests for this PR which aren't particular to the unpining of `scipy`:

- Pyspark pinning version in Windows. There's an issue that it's already been solved in [here](https://github.com/apache/spark/pull/53546) however there isn't a new release with the fix yet.
- Removes Ray testing in Windows since it's still in beta in [Ray 2.53.0](https://docs.ray.io/en/latest/ray-overview/installation.html)
- Scipy stills needs pin down for Python 3.9 since they dropped support in [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20373)
- Updates fugue version to support Ray latest versions
- Installs same in Ray as in all for testing
- Adds Ray session config for testing (without this MacOS with Python 3.9 always hangs)

pullRequest.title: docs: translate Spanish comment to English in SimpleExponentialSmoothing notebook
pullRequest.body:
Fixes #1118

## Summary

Translates a Spanish comment in the `SimpleExponentialSmoothing` notebook to English for consistency with the rest of the codebase.

**Change:**
- `#plt.savefig("Gráfico de Densidad y qq")` → `#plt.savefig("Density and QQ Plot")`
This improves accessibility for non-Spanish-speaking contributors and makes the notebook consistent with the project's English-language documentation.
